system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to attempt to push a creature within 1 Space of you. Make an Athletics Check contested by the target’s Martial Check.

Success: You push the creature 1 Space away from you or to its left or right.

Success (each 5): Push up to 1 additional Space.

Knock Prone: After the result, you can choose to reduce the total distance the target is pushed by 1 Space to knock them Prone instead.

DC Tip: If you shove a creature into a target, follow the rules for hitting the target in the Collision section.

You can spend 1 AP to grant another creature a d8 Help Die that lasts until the start of your next turn. Upon granting the Help Die, you must declare which creature you’re Helping and the type of Check you’ll be aiding them with while meeting the following conditions:

  • Attack: You declare 1 target for the Attack. You must be within 1 Space of the Attacker or the target of the Attack. While the Help Die lasts, it can be added to an Attack made against the target.

  • Skill or Trade Check: You declare a type of Skill or Trade Check. You describe how you’re Helping them and must do so with a Skill or Trade that you have at least 1 Mastery Level in. You can use the same Skill or Trade or a different one.

The Help Die can only be used to aid the type of Check declared and disappears once it is used.

Multiple Help Penalty: Once you take the Help Action, each time you take the Help Action again before the end of your turn, your Help Die decays by 1 step, to a minimum of a d4 (d8 | d6 | d4). These Help Dice only decay when using the Help Action. Help Die granted by other sources (such as the Sword Maneuver) decay independently of any Help Dice grant through the Help Action.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to recall or discern some information about a creature that you can see or hear. Make a DC 10 Knowledge Check.

Success: You learn a piece of lore about the creature.

Success (each 5): You learn 1 creature statistic (PD, AD, Attacks, Abilities, Resistances, Vulnerabilities, Immunities, etc.).

If you move at least 2 Spaces in a straight line, you can spend 1 AP to attempt to Tackle a creature that’s your size or smaller.

Make an Athletics Check contested by the target's Martial Check.

Success: You Grapple the target and you both move 1 Space in the same direction you were moving then immediately fall Prone.

Success (each 5): +1 Space moved.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to move through a Space occupied by a hostile creature that’s within 1 size of you.

Make a contested Martial Check against the target.

Success: You can move through the creature’s Space as if it were Difficult Terrain (Slowed while moving through the area).

Success (5): You can move through the creature's space without it counting as Difficult Terrain.

You must spend 1 AP at the start of each of your turns to Sustain an effect until the start of your next turn.

You can't Sustain an effect while Dazed. When you become Dazed, any effect you’re Sustaining ends. You can choose to end the effect at anytime for free.

Outside of Combat: You can only Sustain 1 effect at a time outside of Combat. If you attempt to Sustain a second effect while already Sustaining a first, the first effect immediately ends.

You can spend 1 AP to perform 1 of the following object interactions:

  • Drink a Potion or administer a Potion to another creature.

  • Attempt to lock or unlock a Lock.

  • Make a Trickery Check to activate or disable a trap or other mechanism.

  • Transfer an item to or from another creature (only 1 of the two creatures spends 1 AP).

You can spend 1 AP to make a Trickery Check Contested by the target’s Insight Check.

Success: The next Attack against the target before the start of your next turn has ADV and deals +1 damage.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to attempt to taunt a creature that can see or hear you within 10 Spaces.

Make an Influence Check contested by the target’s Charisma Save.

Success: The target is Taunted by you for 1 Round.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to intimidate a creature that can see or hear you within 10 Spaces.

Make an Intimidation Check contested by the target’s Charisma Save.

Success: The target is Intimidated by you until the end of your next turn.

When you perform a Jump, you can spend 1 AP to increase your Jump Distance for the Jump.

Make a DC 10 Acrobatics Check.

  • Failure: Your Jump Distance increases by 1.

  • Success: Your Jump Distance increases by 2.

  • Success (each 5): +1 Jump Distance.

DC Tip: Remember, your Long Jump Distance equals your Jump Distance in Spaces, and your High Jump distance equals your Jump Distance in feet.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to locate 1 or more Hidden creatures and concealed objects within your Line of Sight.

  • Hidden Creatures: You attempt to locate any Hidden creatures in the area. Make an Awareness Check against the Stealth Check of any Hidden creatures.

    Success: You know the location of any Hidden creature whose Stealth Check you beat until the end of your turn. Knowing a creature’s location does not grant you the ability to see it.

  • Hidden Objects: You attempt to locate any Hidden objects in the area. Make an Awareness Check against the DC to discover any concealed objects (such as traps, secret doors, or hidden items).

    Success: You discover the location of any Hidden object whose discovery DC you beat.

Without obstruction, a PC’s line of sight could reach the horizon. You might ask your PC to make their Awareness Check with DisADV to detect a creature that’s far away, such as beyond 10 Spaces.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to beguile a Beast that can see or hear you. Make an Animal Check contested by the target’s Charisma Save.

Success: The animal is Taunted by you for 1 minute (Repeated Save) or until you target it with a harmful Attack, Spell, or other effect.

Success (5): It’s also Impaired.

Success (10): It’s also Charmed.

Make a DC 10 Medicine Check.

Success: You stop its Bleeding or Stabilize it (your choice).

Success (each 5): The creature gains +1 Temp HP.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to discern the course of actions a creature might take on its next turn.

Make an Insight Check contested by the target’s Trickery or Influence Check (its choice).

Success: You learn the target’s emotional state and whether it plans to make an Attack, cast a Spell, or flee combat during its next turn.

Success (5): You know who the creature is likely to target with a harmful ability.

Success (10): You know which ability the creature plans to use.

If the target is a PC, its player tells you what the creature plans to do. If the target is an NPC, the GM tells you what the creature plans to do.

system.journal:
Description: A veiled Elven woman in black leathers with a large black crossbow on her back and short black hair swept across half of her face. Her speech is calm and softspoken, though chillingly cold

Tactics: Uses Darkness at the beginning of a fight to hide herself, then Attacks creatures outside of the darkness with her ranged Attacks. When enemies get close, she attacks with her Dagger and then flees. When they line up, she uses a Piercing Shot to hit as many as possible.

Lore: Raven, a Rogue, is the leader of The Shadows, a rather disreputable adventuring group. She and her companions have been accused of stealing glory from other adventurers who never return from contracts.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to throw an object or a creature you have Grappled.

Object

The target is thrown up to a number of Spaces away as shown in the Throwing Table. For the purposes of throwing, your minimum Might is 1. The total distance thrown is halved if thrown vertically.

Throw at a Target: When you throw an object at a target it’s considered a Ranged Martial Attack with an Improvised Weapon against the target’s PD.

  • Hit: The target takes 1 damage of a type determined by the GM.

  • Miss: The thrown object continues traveling up to your maximum throwing distance.

DC Tip: When you throw Weapons (including an Improvised Weapon) that lack the Toss or Thrown Properties as part of an Attack, you make the Attack with DisADV.

Creature

Make an Athletics Check against the target’s Might Save.

  • Check Success: The target is thrown up to a number of Spaces away as shown in the Throwing Table (ending the Grapple).

For the purposes of throwing, your minimum Might is 1. The total distance thrown is halved if thrown vertically upward.

Throw at a Target: When you throw the creature at a target, you compare your Athletics Check against the target’s PD.

  • Hit: The thrown creature and the target share the Collision damage (each take half).

  • Miss: The thrown creature continues traveling to your maximum throwing distance.

Throwing Table

Size

Spaces Thrown

2+ Sizes smaller

2 x Might

1 Size smaller

Might

Same Size

1/2 Might

Larger

Impossible

Example: A Medium sized creature with a Might of 0 (minimum Might of 1) could throw a Tiny object up to 2 Spaces, a Small object 1 Space, and Medium object 1 Space (rounded up from 0.5). Conversely, if that creature had a Might of 6, they could throw a Tiny object 12 Spaces, a Small object 6 Spaces, and a Medium object 3 Spaces.

System Info: In the DC20 system, throwing is not a basic action and has its own section in the rules. For automation purposes, I added it to the actions.

Reaction: You can spend an amount of AP and 1 or more MP (up to your Mana Spend Limit) to attempt to stop the Caster’s MP Effect. The amount of AP spent is equal to the base AP cost of the MP Effect you are trying to stop.

Contest

Make a Spell Check contested by the Spell Check or Spell Attack that the Caster makes to cast their MP Effect.

If the Caster’s MP Effect doesn’t require a Check, they have to make a Spell Check as a Reaction as part of the Spell Duel.

For the purposes of the Spell Duel, the Caster and Challenger each gain a bonus to their Check equal to twice the MP spent they spent respectively.

Beta Note: Since enemies don’t have MP costs for their MP Effects you can assume that their MP Effects cost an amount of MP equal to half their Level (rounded up).

Outcomes

Success: The Caster’s MP Effect fails and has no effect.

Failure: The Caster’s MP Effect succeeds and takes effect. The Check bonus gained from MP spent doesn’t apply to the MP Effect’s Success and Failure Statements.

Tie: The Caster’s MP Effect fails, has no effect, and you each roll on the @UUID[Compendium.dc20rpg.roll-tables.RollTable.HxkcpDT6pEOIfYUk]{Wild Magic Surge Table}. The effect from the table lasts for 1 Round.

Resources Lost: Whatever the result, each creature still spends all AP, MP, or other resources as normal.

Example: A Caster makes an MP based Spell Attack and a Challenger attempts to stop it. The Caster’s Spell Attack cost 1 AP and 2 MP. The Challenger must spend 1 AP and at least 1 MP to attempt to stop the Spell Attack. The Caster makes an Attack Check (since its a Spell Attack) and the Challenger makes a Spell Check. The Caster gets a +4 bonus to their Attack Check (2 MP spent) and the Challenger gets a +2 bonus to their Spell Check (1 MP spent). Should the Caster win the Contest, their MP Effect takes effect but they don’t include the +4 bonus from MP spent to determine if they score a Heavy Hit or higher.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to attempt to push a creature within 1 Space of you. Make an Athletics Check contested by the target’s Martial Check.

Success: You push the creature 1 Space away from you or to its left or right.

Success (each 5): Push up to 1 additional Space.

Knock Prone: After the result, you can choose to reduce the total distance the target is pushed by 1 Space to knock them Prone instead.

DC Tip: If you shove a creature into a target, follow the rules for hitting the target in the Collision section.

You can spend 1 AP to grant another creature a d8 Help Die that lasts until the start of your next turn. Upon granting the Help Die, you must declare which creature you’re Helping and the type of Check you’ll be aiding them with while meeting the following conditions:

  • Attack: You declare 1 target for the Attack. You must be within 1 Space of the Attacker or the target of the Attack. While the Help Die lasts, it can be added to an Attack made against the target.

  • Skill or Trade Check: You declare a type of Skill or Trade Check. You describe how you’re Helping them and must do so with a Skill or Trade that you have at least 1 Mastery Level in. You can use the same Skill or Trade or a different one.

The Help Die can only be used to aid the type of Check declared and disappears once it is used.

Multiple Help Penalty: Once you take the Help Action, each time you take the Help Action again before the end of your turn, your Help Die decays by 1 step, to a minimum of a d4 (d8 | d6 | d4). These Help Dice only decay when using the Help Action. Help Die granted by other sources (such as the Sword Maneuver) decay independently of any Help Dice grant through the Help Action.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to recall or discern some information about a creature that you can see or hear. Make a DC 10 Knowledge Check.

Success: You learn a piece of lore about the creature.

Success (each 5): You learn 1 creature statistic (PD, AD, Attacks, Abilities, Resistances, Vulnerabilities, Immunities, etc.).

If you move at least 2 Spaces in a straight line, you can spend 1 AP to attempt to Tackle a creature that’s your size or smaller.

Make an Athletics Check contested by the target's Martial Check.

Success: You Grapple the target and you both move 1 Space in the same direction you were moving then immediately fall Prone.

Success (each 5): +1 Space moved.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to move through a Space occupied by a hostile creature that’s within 1 size of you.

Make a contested Martial Check against the target.

Success: You can move through the creature’s Space as if it were Difficult Terrain (Slowed while moving through the area).

Success (5): You can move through the creature's space without it counting as Difficult Terrain.

You must spend 1 AP at the start of each of your turns to Sustain an effect until the start of your next turn.

You can't Sustain an effect while Dazed. When you become Dazed, any effect you’re Sustaining ends. You can choose to end the effect at anytime for free.

Outside of Combat: You can only Sustain 1 effect at a time outside of Combat. If you attempt to Sustain a second effect while already Sustaining a first, the first effect immediately ends.

You can spend 1 AP to perform 1 of the following object interactions:

  • Drink a Potion or administer a Potion to another creature.

  • Attempt to lock or unlock a Lock.

  • Make a Trickery Check to activate or disable a trap or other mechanism.

  • Transfer an item to or from another creature (only 1 of the two creatures spends 1 AP).

You can spend 1 AP to make a Trickery Check Contested by the target’s Insight Check.

Success: The next Attack against the target before the start of your next turn has ADV and deals +1 damage.

effects.name: Feint
effects.description: The next Attack against you has ADV and deals +1 damage.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to attempt to taunt a creature that can see or hear you within 10 Spaces.

Make an Influence Check contested by the target’s Charisma Save.

Success: The target is Taunted by you for 1 Round.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to intimidate a creature that can see or hear you within 10 Spaces.

Make an Intimidation Check contested by the target’s Charisma Save.

Success: The target is Intimidated by you until the end of your next turn.

When you perform a Jump, you can spend 1 AP to increase your Jump Distance for the Jump.

Make a DC 10 Acrobatics Check.

  • Failure: Your Jump Distance increases by 1.

  • Success: Your Jump Distance increases by 2.

  • Success (each 5): +1 Jump Distance.

DC Tip: Remember, your Long Jump Distance equals your Jump Distance in Spaces, and your High Jump distance equals your Jump Distance in feet.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to locate 1 or more Hidden creatures and concealed objects within your Line of Sight.

  • Hidden Creatures: You attempt to locate any Hidden creatures in the area. Make an Awareness Check against the Stealth Check of any Hidden creatures.

    Success: You know the location of any Hidden creature whose Stealth Check you beat until the end of your turn. Knowing a creature’s location does not grant you the ability to see it.

  • Hidden Objects: You attempt to locate any Hidden objects in the area. Make an Awareness Check against the DC to discover any concealed objects (such as traps, secret doors, or hidden items).

    Success: You discover the location of any Hidden object whose discovery DC you beat.

Without obstruction, a PC’s line of sight could reach the horizon. You might ask your PC to make their Awareness Check with DisADV to detect a creature that’s far away, such as beyond 10 Spaces.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to beguile a Beast that can see or hear you. Make an Animal Check contested by the target’s Charisma Save.

Success: The animal is Taunted by you for 1 minute (Repeated Save) or until you target it with a harmful Attack, Spell, or other effect.

Success (5): It’s also Impaired.

Success (10): It’s also Charmed.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to hide an object on yourself or in nearby foliage, debris, or decor to render it Hidden.

Make a contested Trickery Check against the Passive Awareness of creatures that can see you.

Success: The object is Hidden from any creature whose Passive Awareness you beat.

You can spend 1 AP to touch a creature and tend to its wounds.

Make a DC 10 Medicine Check.

Success: You stop its Bleeding or Stabilize it (your choice).

Success (each 5): The creature gains +1 Temp HP.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to discern the course of actions a creature might take on its next turn.

Make an Insight Check contested by the target’s Trickery or Influence Check (its choice).

Success: You learn the target’s emotional state and whether it plans to make an Attack, cast a Spell, or flee combat during its next turn.

Success (5): You know who the creature is likely to target with a harmful ability.

Success (10): You know which ability the creature plans to use.

If the target is a PC, its player tells you what the creature plans to do. If the target is an NPC, the GM tells you what the creature plans to do.

system.journal:
Description: A stocky Dwarf woman with a black, wispy beard. Her eyes are a dark void and her voice is deep and echoing.

Tactics: Prioritizes healing Bloodied allies and dealing damage to Bloodied enemies. Will try to shut down enemy Spells whenever possible.

Lore: Dusk, a Cleric, is the support of The Shadows, a rather disreputable adventuring group.

Token Create with TokenStamp 2.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to throw an object or a creature you have Grappled.

Object

The target is thrown up to a number of Spaces away as shown in the Throwing Table. For the purposes of throwing, your minimum Might is 1. The total distance thrown is halved if thrown vertically.

Throw at a Target: When you throw an object at a target it’s considered a Ranged Martial Attack with an Improvised Weapon against the target’s PD.

  • Hit: The target takes 1 damage of a type determined by the GM.

  • Miss: The thrown object continues traveling up to your maximum throwing distance.

DC Tip: When you throw Weapons (including an Improvised Weapon) that lack the Toss or Thrown Properties as part of an Attack, you make the Attack with DisADV.

Creature

Make an Athletics Check against the target’s Might Save.

  • Check Success: The target is thrown up to a number of Spaces away as shown in the Throwing Table (ending the Grapple).

For the purposes of throwing, your minimum Might is 1. The total distance thrown is halved if thrown vertically upward.

Throw at a Target: When you throw the creature at a target, you compare your Athletics Check against the target’s PD.

  • Hit: The thrown creature and the target share the Collision damage (each take half).

  • Miss: The thrown creature continues traveling to your maximum throwing distance.

Throwing Table

Size

Spaces Thrown

2+ Sizes smaller

2 x Might

1 Size smaller

Might

Same Size

1/2 Might

Larger

Impossible

Example: A Medium sized creature with a Might of 0 (minimum Might of 1) could throw a Tiny object up to 2 Spaces, a Small object 1 Space, and Medium object 1 Space (rounded up from 0.5). Conversely, if that creature had a Might of 6, they could throw a Tiny object 12 Spaces, a Small object 6 Spaces, and a Medium object 3 Spaces.

System Info: In the DC20 system, throwing is not a basic action and has its own section in the rules. For automation purposes, I added it to the actions.

10 Space Aura

Creatures of your choice within the Aura take +1 damage from Attacks while Bloodied.

System Tip: Not Automated.
You can spend 1 AP to make 1 Attack Check.
You can spend 1 AP to impose DisADV on Opportunity Attacks made against you until the start of your next turn.

system.description: You can spend 2 AP to become immune to Opportunity Attacks until the start of your next turn.

system.description: You can spend 1 AP to impose DisADV on the next Attack or Grapple made against you before the start of your next turn.

system.description: You can spend 2 AP to impose DisADV on all Attacks or Grapples made against you until the start of your next turn.

effects.description: You impose DisADV on all Attacks or Grapples made against you until the start of your next turn.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to attempt to Hide from 1 or more creatures that can’t see you (Unseen).

Make a Stealth Check against the opposing creature’(s) Passive Awareness.

Success: You become Hidden from creatures whose Passive Awareness you beat (making you Unseen and Unheard by them).

You remain Hidden until you make a noise louder than a whisper, make an Attack, cast a Spell with a Verbal Component, or a creature takes the Search Action and successfully locates you.

See @UUID[Compendium.dc20rpg.rules.JournalEntry.HNPA8Fd7ynirYUBq.JournalEntryPage.a0d67820b3677829]{Hidden Creatures} (@UUID[Compendium.dc20rpg.rules.JournalEntry.HNPA8Fd7ynirYUBq.JournalEntryPage.6396da38cde5e264]{Unseen}, @UUID[Compendium.dc20rpg.rules.JournalEntry.HNPA8Fd7ynirYUBq.JournalEntryPage.8c1b9e075707a2ce]{Unheard}, @UUID[Compendium.dc20rpg.rules.JournalEntry.HNPA8Fd7ynirYUBq.JournalEntryPage.38850187f9364dd3]{Hidden}) for more on hiding.

When you take the Hide Action and beat an enemy’s Passive Awareness, you only become Hidden from that creature because it can’t see or hear you (you’re both Unseen and Unheard). However, if your Stealth Check beats one enemy’s Awareness Score but not another’s, then you’re Hidden from the first enemy, but you’re not Hidden from the second enemy.

effects.name: Hidden
effects.description: You become Hidden from creatures whose Passive Awareness you beat.

system.description: You can spend 1 or more AP to cast a Spell that you know. If the Spell has a Mana Point requirement, you must spend that much MP to cast the Spell.

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to uncover a concealed object on a creature, a secret compartment, or the intended function of a mechanism within 1 Space of you.

  • Concealed Objects: You can attempt to uncover any objects concealed on a creature.

    Make an Investigation Check contested by the target’s Trickery Check.

    Success: You know the location of any concealed object on the creature.

  • Secret Compartments: You can attempt to uncover any secret compartments. Make an Investigation Check against the discovery DC of any secret compartments.

    Success: You discover the location of any secret compartments whose discovery DC you beat.

  • Discern Mechanism: You can attempt to discern the functionality of a mechanism (the effect of a trap, how to open a secret door, or activate a device). Make an Investigation Check.

    Success: You learn how the mechanism works and the methods to activate and disable it (if any).

Prerequisite: Martial Path

Trigger: A creature you can see within your Melee Range uses its movement to leave your Melee Range, draws a Weapon or Spell Focus, picks an item up off the ground, or takes the Object Action.

Reaction: You can spend 1 AP to make a Melee Martial Attack against the provoking creature.

DC Tip: You can spend additional AP to gain ADV or to perform Maneuvers with the Attack.
For the purpose of the Spell Duels, your MP Effects have a +2 bonus to their Check.

Using a free hand, you can spend 1 AP to attempt to Grapple another creature within 1 Space.

Make an Athletics Check contested by the opposing creature’s Martial Check.

You or the target may have ADV or DisADV on your Check based on each other’s size (see @UUID[Compendium.dc20rpg.rules.JournalEntry.HNPA8Fd7ynirYUBq.JournalEntryPage.ae17b973b3872de1]{Moving & Grappling Creatures}).

  • Success: The creature is Grappled by you.

Moving the Target

You can move the target to any Space adjacent to you by spending your own Movement to do so. Alternatively, when you move, you can move the target with you, but you’re considered Slowed (Every 1 Space you move costs an extra 1 Space of movement).

Multiple Grapplers: If you attempt to move or throw a target (see Throw Creature Maneuver) that’s also Grappled by 1 or more other creatures, you must make a Contested Athletics Check against all the other Grapplers.

  • Success: You move or throw the target as normal and break the Grapple of all other creatures who were also Grappling that target.

Ending a Grapple

Escape Grapple: The target can spend 1 AP to attempt to free itself from the Grapple. When it does, it makes an Acrobatics or Athletics Check contested by your Athletics Check. Success: The Grapple immediately ends.

Incapacitated Grappler: If you become Incapacitated, the Grapple immediately ends.

Forced Movement: If an effect attempts to forcibly move the target beyond your reach, you make the Check or Save instead of the target. If the effect targets both you and the target, you make 1 Check or Save for both of you.

  • Success: The targets of the effect are not moved.

  • Failure: The Grapple immediately ends, and the targets of the effect are moved.

Falling: If the target begins falling (and you don’t), the Grapple ends if you can’t carry the target’s weight. If you can carry its weight, you hold it suspended in the air.

You can spend 1 AP to make an Attack Check Contested by the target's Athletics, Acrobatics, or Trickery Check (target’s choice).

The target has ADV if they’re holding the object with 2 hands. You have DisADV if the target is larger than you. You can’t Disarm a creature that’s 2 Sizes larger than you.

Success: The targeted object falls into an unoccupied space of your choice within 1 Space of the creature.

system.description:
You can spend 1 AP to move up to your Speed in Spaces (default of 5).

You choose where to move, and can break up your movement by moving before and after taking a different Action.

You can’t end your turn in a Space occupied by another creature.

A creature with 4 AP can take the Move Action to move 1 Space, take the Attack Action, move 1 Space again (2 total Spaces), take the Attack Action again, and then move 2 Spaces again (4 total Spaces).

text.content:
For GMs

This adventure is designed to be setting agnostic and is presented in a way to ease those dropping this introductory story into any world.

Party Level: This adventure is written for 1st-level PCs, though it can be tailored to fit slightly higher-level characters with a few tweaks.

In the adventure, the party goes after their first target, a monster you and your players get to make up together. However, they’ll face adversity in the form of their rivals, The Shadows, and an unexpected threat. Once they complete their mission, they must fight off their rivals before they can steal the party’s credit for slaying their mark... and then, fight an even bigger monster that’s been stalking the swamplands: the Krasis.

For Players

You are adventurers who have made your way to the city of Wildfang in hopes of joining the Bloodsworn, a renowned Hunters Guild. A successful trial hunt will earn your membership and allow you to climb through their ranks, one contract at a time. Membership comes more lucrative jobs and access to services only the guild provides. But before you can even attempt your hunt, you’ll need to prove yourselves to the guild’s leader. Just watch out for any rival adventuring parties trying to steal your glory!

Supplemental Information

Sidebar: Reading a Settlement Statblock

Below is Wildfang’s settlement statblock. Settlement statblocks describe the fundamental information pertaining to a settlement, just as a creature’s statblock might for a creature.

Level: This represents its relative size and economic capacity in most settings, though certain settings may have different standard ranges for the size of its settlements. Mechanically, it can be used to approximate the highest level NPC that might be found in the settlement, and the maximum Magic Power of items easily purchased there - though typically the Magic Power of an individual Magic Property does not exceed half of the settlement’s level.

Government: The name of the governing entity, and a description of their form of power.

Population: Total population, with some form of breakdown of the population by ancestry.

Threats: Major threats facing the settlement.

Terrain: The biomes immediately surrounding the settlement.

Significant NPCs: The most significant NPCs of the settlement, especially as it might pertain to adventurers.

Background: Information explaining the history or recent events of the settlement, providing the background and reasoning for its more distinctive elements.

Character: The most distinctive and apparent cultural elements of the settlement, including the behavior of its inhabitants, its architecture, and overall atmosphere; anything visitors might notice as they walk its streets and talk to its locals.

Other: Additional entries may be listed here for any feature of the settlement which is uniquely important.

Key Moments

  • The PCs are in the Bloodsworn Guildhall, awaiting a meeting with the guild’s Warden.

  • The players introduce their PCs and develop the party dynamic.

  • The players describe the monster on the bounty flyer they have

  • The players learn about their rival adventuring party and decide how they met them.

The following are the key scenes for this adventure.

Sidebar: Using the Adventure Outline

Each Scene in the adventure outline for The Hunted has three components:

  • A brief description of what happens in the Scene.

  • Tutorial Importance: How this Scene teaches you and your players about playing DC20. Most adventures will not serve as a tutorial and so will lack this component.

  • Narrative Importance: How this Scene pertains to the narrative of the adventure, and thus how the narrative might lose cohesion if the Scene is skipped.

If there’s a Scene you don’t think will play well at your table for any reason, you can use the outline to keep the Scene’s important elements in mind as you change the adventure to suit your table. You could alter the Scene while keeping the important elements intact, or remove those elements from this Scene but make up for them in other Scenes or in a Scene of your own creation.

You can also refer back to the outline in case your players go “off-track,” and it becomes difficult to ascertain how to get them back “on-track.” You can insert elements into the new Scenes your players have created to fulfill the purpose of Scenes they might not experience, and can also quickly find which future Scene in the adventure is the most plausible re-entry point back into the adventure’s narrative.

Scenes will be broken up in the following ways. Each color means something and helps to organize this adventure to both be easier to prep and run live during a session.

Key Moments: Each Scene starts with this text, which gives you a summary of the important moments of the entire scene.

Plain Text: This text walks the GM through the adventure during prep and when you run the game. It efficiently says what things come next, explains mechanics, and should not be read out loud.

Orange Box Text: This text gives information for rules and mechanics or reminders about the adventure.

Green Box Text: Our read-aloud text, which you as the GM can read out word-for-word (or add your own twist to)

Blue Box Text: This is spoken NPC dialogue in that character’s voice. It has a prompt in brackets for what the type of voice is, and a prompt for tone in parentheses. You read this out loud just like you do the Green Text, but this is spoken as an NPC for the GM to better embody or roleplay.

  • There might be a section of Blue Box Text with “Talking Points”. These sections are for when there’s a back and forth dialogue with the PCs. These have a line in-scene delivering the information (the “talking points”) with a bullet point underneath to summarize the dialogue

GM Prep Text: All you need to do to prep this adventure is read the Key Moments, Plain Text, and Orange Box Text. Running the Session Text: During the session you only need to read the Green Text and Blue text out loud to the players, and then read the Plain Text to yourself to give guidance and reminders along the way.

Supplemental Information

Sidebar: Reading a Settlement Statblock

Below is Wildfang’s settlement statblock. Settlement statblocks describe the fundamental information pertaining to a settlement, just as a creature’s statblock might for a creature.

Level: This represents its relative size and economic capacity in most settings, though certain settings may have different standard ranges for the size of its settlements. Mechanically, it can be used to approximate the highest level NPC that might be found in the settlement, and the maximum Magic Power of items easily purchased there - though typically the Magic Power of an individual Magic Property does not exceed half of the settlement’s level.

Government: The name of the governing entity, and a description of their form of power.

Population: Total population, with some form of breakdown of the population by ancestry.

Threats: Major threats facing the settlement.

Terrain: The biomes immediately surrounding the settlement.

Significant NPCs: The most significant NPCs of the settlement, especially as it might pertain to adventurers.

Background: Information explaining the history or recent events of the settlement, providing the background and reasoning for its more distinctive elements.

Character: The most distinctive and apparent cultural elements of the settlement, including the behavior of its inhabitants, its architecture, and overall atmosphere; anything visitors might notice as they walk its streets and talk to its locals.

Other: Additional entries may be listed here for any feature of the settlement which is uniquely important.

Wildfang

Level: 10

Government: Administrator (Autocrat appointed by coalition of nearby sovereign powers)

Population: Around 25,000 (40% human, all ancestries represented)

Threats: Militarization, monstrous incursions, rogue adventurers

Terrain: Arid desert west, otherwise surrounded by forest, with mountains south and east

Soukina Haddad: Affable female angelborn Administrator. Current Administrator of Wildfang, served by deputies appointed each by a different power of the coalition.

Eugene Kavouri: Grizzled male human. Warden of the Bloodsworn Hunters’ Guild, experienced monster hunter.

Kereg: Mysterious male gremlin broker. Introduces himself to anyone of interest in the city, can find just about any item, service, or information someone needs... for a price.

Background

Wildfang was founded only a few decades ago from a compact between the ruling classes of various surrounding lands: a safe haven in the middle of monster-strewn wilderness, beholden to no one lord or kingdom, and serving as a foothold from which to curb the monstrous threat of the territory. Initially a joint military town, conflicts between the different military factions caused each government to instead rely more and more on mercenary aid, until the mercenaries themselves founded the Bloodsworn Hunters’ Guild.

Character

The people of Wildfang were drawn not just from the various surrounding territories, but from all over the world, with many believing their fortunes could be made either in the slaying of monsters or in endeavors which indirectly profit from it. The eclectic architecture of the city reflects this: rows of terrace homes are interspersed with stand-alone structures, and different combinations of dark mountain stone, forest wood, and brightly colored mud bricks result in a striking and often overwhelming impression as one walks Wildfang’s streets.

Bloodsworn

The Bloodsworn Hunters’ Guild is the largest guild of monster hunters in the city, and the heart of Wildfang’s community. They are contracted by the nobility of surrounding territories to defend their lands from monstrous threats. The most elite of members earn the title of Reaper.

The Guildhall, also known as the Bloodsworn Keep, is an imposing, sprawling structure of stone and wood situated atop a foothill on the outskirts of Wildfang with a good view of the city. Being set against the dense forest used for training drills makes its blood-red banners all the more striking.

As a university’s students and faculty are to a college town, the Bloodsworn are to Wildfang: the guild is deeply intertwined with the goings-on of the city. While the Bloodsworn have no direct political authority, their leaders have much sway throughout the city

Isolated city known for its high population of monsterhunting adventurers of every skillset, with culture and commerce structured around this profession.

During the initial scenes and resolution of the adventure, players might want to describe how their characters move through the city of Wildfang or ask questions about different elements of the city to better ground themselves into the world. The details provided in the settlement statblock are primarily to support you should you want or need to describe the city further than each scene does in its read-aloud text.

Additionally, The Hunted is intended as an introduction to DC 20, and so information for the city of Wildfang beyond the scope of what is needed for the adventure can also be used for continuing the campaign past the adventure’s resolution, whether as the foundation of a new setting or by slotting an adaptation of Wildfang into a pre-existing setting.

Placing the Adventure in Another Setting

The Hunted can easily be adapted to fit into many settings, and thus the city of Wildfang can be modified to suit the assumptions of your setting of choice. While features such as terrain, key NPCs, and the character of Wildfang as a city are provided, all that is pertinent to the adventure is the existence of an adventuring or monster hunting guild, its grizzled leader, and a swamp a few hours by wagon from the settlement. All other details, including the settlement’s name, are easily changed with little to no bearing on the adventure.

The Foundation of a New Setting

If you as a GM don’t have a firm setting concept in mind, you may find it helpful to start just with what’s provided in Wildfang’s statblock and gradually expand outwards. Many new GMs believe they must spend months or years crafting a perfect world before placing their players into it, however some of the best campaigns started with only one settlement’s details firmly established, then slowly expanding from there.

If you wish to use Wildfang in this way, here are some questions you may choose to explore as you develop the setting both in and around it:

  • Who are the various sovereign powers who joined together to form Wildfang?

  • How many of them are there, where are each located in relation to Wildfang, and how far is each of their nearest settlements or seats of power?

  • What might their settlement statblocks look like, and what interests do these powers have - especially interests which might pertain to Wildfang itself?

  • What kind of cultural elements have taken hold in Wildfang, whether these elements are wholesale imports from neighboring regions, adaptations and hybrids born from a confluence of such elements, or entirely new and unique to this young city?

  • Such cultural elements can include: cuisine, faiths, fashion, hobbies, holidays, languages, gender roles, military traditions, rites of passage, slang, social hierarchies, taboos, trade practices, etc.

  • Why is the region around Wildfang so dangerous and monster-laden?

  • Who held these lands previously, and how long ago?

  • Did some god or powerful mage curse this land? Was some great battle once fought here which left the terrain magically scarred in such a way that bred monsters?

The Shadows are a rough adventuring party that have quickly risen in notoriety due to their successes through underhanded tactics. There are rumors that they have criminal contacts and have completed some illegal jobs for gold or favors.

Appears in Scene 4.

Though now destroyed, Marshall Keep was once an outpost at the edge of a kingdom’s borders to keep monsters out of its land. The castle and many like it were overrun by monsters growing faster than they could be beaten back, leading to surrounding kingdoms banding together to establish Wildfang as a town of monster hunters in the middle of the wilderness to keep their numbers manageable

Eugene (Male): Human Warden of the Bloodsworn

  • Voice: Firm and raspy.

  • Appearance: A grizzled man with a wrinkled face and gray slicked-back hair. He wears decorative yet functional leather armor with weapons strapped in several easy to access locations. His informal and laid back demeanor belies his true strength.

  • Background: A former Reaper of the Bloodsworn, Eugene is now well past his prime and focused on leading the next generation. His many years of experience still make him a formidable combatant though.

  • Statistics: 5 Prime Mod, 25 HP, 18 PD, 18 AD, Longsword (+10 Melee Martial, 5 Slashing)

@UUID[Actor.zPt9s0hUx0ReAJyc]{Raven (Female)}: Elven Leader of The Shadows

  • Voice: Calm and cold.

  • Appearance: An Elven woman in dark leathers with a large ashwood crossbow on her back and short black hair swept across half of her thinly veiled face.

  • Background: Raven, a Rogue, is the leader of The Shadows, a rather disreputable adventuring group. She and her bedfellows have been accused of stealing glory from other adventurers who never return from contracts.

  • Statistics: Use the Raven (Crossbow Rogue) statblock.

@UUID[Actor.cEJYrnrTLGeu2hbU]{Gruff (Male)}: Halfing Giantborn “Face” of The Shadows.

  • Voice: Snarky and squeaky.

  • Appearance: Gruff is a small Halfling Giantborn man with a flowing shirt that’s left open down to his navel. He’s usually smirking with an air of superiority.

  • Background: Gruff, a Monk, is the second in command of The Shadows, a rather disreputable adventuring group. He joined the group with his brother Buff, the group’s muscle. Gruff got his father’s small size and sly charm.

  • Statistics: Use the Gruff (Monk) statblock.

@UUID[Actor.QNQV1ZVnXafFNyVa]{Buff (Male)}: Giantborn Halfling Muscle of The Shadows

  • Voice: Deep and slow.

  • Appearance: A large pile of muscles equipped with thick furs and iron knuckles, Buff is a Giantborn Halfling usually sporting a vacant stare with a slack jaw. He’s thick-skinned as a rhinoceros and as smart as one, too.

  • Background: Buff is the Barbarian muscle of The Shadows, a rather disreputable adventuring group. He joined the group with his brother Gruff, the group’s second-in-command. Buff got his mother’s large size and slow wit.

  • Statistics: Use the Buff (Grappler Barbarian) statblock.

The Bloodsworn Guildhall is described to the players. Each player introduces their PC by explaining how they behave in the Guild Hall before meeting the Warden, then the players work together to craft three things:

  • Their party’s history and dynamic, including why they want to join the Bloodsworn.

  • The name, image, and abilities of a monster depicted on a Bloodsworn bounty flyer they found.

  • How they drew the attention of The Shadows, their rival adventuring party.

The players are informed of three members of The Shadows, and that their bounty flyer states that one of them was the last person to see the monster.

Tutorial Importance: Facilitates players and GM working together to collectively craft and tell a story first about the player characters and NPCs, then further facilitates making something to put in the world which does not directly relate to their characters (the monster).

Narrative Importance: Establishes the setting, the history between the players’ party and The Shadows, and the monster contract that the party must fulfill through the Bloodsworn Hunters’ Guild.

The PCs meet the Bloodsworn’s Warden and engage in a Social Challenge to prove that they’re capable of completing their chosen bounty. After proving themselves worthy, they’re sent to hunt down the monster from Scene 1.

Tutorial Importance: Teaches how to engage in a Social Challenge and how players can utilize the features and abilities on their PCs’ character sheet.

Narrative Importance: Warden Eugene is introduced and the party gains permission to pursue the bounty

The PCs leave the guildhall and run into two members of The Shadows, who try to bully them into giving up their contract. The leader of The Shadows appears and calls her subordinates away.

Tutorial Importance: Learning the basics of TTRPG roleplay and developing relationships, both good and bad, with the NPCs of the world.

Narrative Importance: Shows that The Shadows want the bounty.

DC Tip: If you want to lengthen the adventure, in between Scenes 3 and 4 is likely the best place to do so. Maybe the party tries to investigate what The Shadows are up to, explore the city, prepare for their adventure through shopping or other means, or talk to Bloodsworn members before leaving Wildfang. Alternatively, they could explore the swamplands near their quarry before successfully tracking and hunting it.

The party travels to go after their target. When they reach the location, they engage in a Combat Challenge to determine how the fight with the monster goes.

Tutorial Importance: Teaches some elements of exploration outside of a settlement, that not all combats need to include rolling for initiative, and how to engage in rapid conflict resolution.

Narrative Importance: The PCs are now hours away from Wildfang and have successfully hunted their quarry.

The PCs are ambushed by their rivals, The Shadows, who try to kill them and take the credit for slaying the bounty.

Tutorial Importance: Teaches the mechanics of a DC20 Combat Encounter.

Narrative Importance: The PCs have defeated their rivals.

DC Tip: If you need to shorten the Adventure because time is running out, you can always combine the 2 combats into 1 by having the final monster show up DURING the fight against the Rivals. Only do this if the PCs are winning against the Rivals. If the fight is close, then you can have the fight against the Rivals be the “final boss fight” instead.

The PCs get a chance to rest and roleplay together.

Tutorial Importance: Teaches how to recover after a conflict using Rest Points, and potentially their class features.

Narrative Importance: The PCs are well rested and ready to head home.

Before the party gets a chance to make it to town, they’re attacked by a creature native to these swamps called a krasis. They must fight the monster and its brood so they can make it back safely to Wildfang and collect their reward.

Tutorial Importance: Teaches how combats with Epic or Legendary Monsters differs from normal Combat Encounters.

Narrative Importance: The PCs get back safely, turn in their bounty, and join the Bloodsworn.

Rules & Reminders

Information the GM should know before running, such as rules or mechanics of a specific Scene.

Reminder about something in the Scene/Adventure that might not matter in the current moment.

We start our adventure in the Bloodsworn Guildhall, a busy place filled with the thrill of the hunt. You hear the clashing of steel from sparring matches, you smell the fresh spring air mix with the hearty food from the mess hall, you see all sorts of hunters telling stories of past successful hunts, or nursing their wounds from failed ones.

You have a contract to slay a monster, but you were just informed that the Warden likes to interview all potential Bloodsworn initiates before their first mission, you now all wait in this massive guild hall to be called back. There are books on all sorts of monsters on the shelves, training dummies to practice on, a tavern to relax at, but now each of you please tell us your character’s name, then describe your character and what they’re currently doing in the Bloodsworn Guildhall.

Guiding the Roleplay

If your players are uncertain of who should go first, you can pick one of them; if you know one is more confident or has experience playing TTRPGs, you can call on them. The Bloodsworn Guildhall has amenities beyond what’s described in the opening text, and you can make this clear to your players - if they ask if there is a facility in the Guildhall to express a certain aspect of their character, you can work with them to make such a facility.

Your group are hopeful initiates to the Bloodsworn, but now lets learn more about your group.

  • How long / what brought you together?

  • What is the group dynamic (in or out of combat)?

  • What is the group name?

  • Who is the leader?

Guiding the Discussion

You as the GM should know the characters the players are bringing beforehand, so feel free to offer suggestions of tie-ins between characters yourself - especially if the players are at a loss. Remember that the group should have been formed long enough to have had an encounter with a rival adventuring party, and so can’t be brand new, but also shouldn’t be so accomplished that it makes little sense for them to still be level 1 adventurers without a reputation. It’s also okay if the party doesn’t have a name or leader - some adventuring parties never have either.

[To the group leader] In your possession is a flyer you found from a Bloodsworn job board for a bounty contract. It depicts a fearsome monster which you hope to convince the Warden you can slay, then follow through on doing so, in order to gain initiation into the guild. What does this monster look like, and why is it so dangerous?

Guiding the Discussion

This monster will be defeated in a Combat Challenge later in the adventure by the party - it should sound like it could pose enough of a threat that complete novices may be bested by it, but not so threatening that it feels anticlimactic when the PCs are able to beat it in a Challenge that doesn’t require a full Combat Encounter. Guide discussion of this monster to try and keep it within these boundaries. If the players need a starting point, you can mention that the flyer says the monster was prowling a swamp.

There’s a rival group of hunters known as The Shadows. Their leader is a stoic rogue named Raven who doesn’t play by the rules.

  • Why are you rivals with this group / why do they not like you?

Guiding the Discussion

You can help the players decide the nature of their rivalry by providing some more information about The Shadows’ members. The rival adventuring party has the exact same number of members as there are Player Characters (minimum of 3 rivals). The group always has Raven (leader) as well as Gruff & Buff (half-Halfling Giantborns). You can add on as many secondary additional members as you need to have their party size match the PCs’ numbers. If there are any additional members beyond the initial three, you can choose to keep them a secret until they later ambush the party, but the party should always have met at least Raven, Buff, and Gruff. Information about each of The Shadows can be found in the NPCs section.

Certain PCs may be engaged in tasks which have a chance of failure or varying levels of success - for instance, PCs may choose to spar each other or Bloodsworn. In some instances, you may be able to simply say a PC succeeds at their task in whatever way you deem fit, but in other instances a player may feel that they should “earn” their success with a roll - especially if in a contest like a bout against another PC. In the case of something like a bout, players can roll contested Attack Checks to determine who wins each bout (no damage incurred).

Here are some other examples of the kinds of activities players may wish to roll Checks for as part of playing their character:

  • Sparring with another character

  • Interacting with an NPC (flirting, antagonizing, questioning, etc.).

  • Searching the surroundings

Alternatively, you may want to wait until Eugene comes out to meet them to call for Checks, as the party will be demonstrating to Eugene that they’re capable of taking on the contract through a Social Challenge which will call for such Checks anyways. The PCs can be left waiting for as little as 5 minutes or as long as an hour before Eugene comes out to meet them.

Explaining Challenges To Your Players:

Challenges are a fun way to resolve dramatic moments. You’ll each describe what your character is attempting to do to impress the Warden of the Bloodsworn, and I’ll determine what Check that corresponds to. You’ll then make a Check against the Challenge DC. Doing well as a group will result in a better outcome.

Challenge Rules

Step 1: Set Parameters

GM sets:

  • Challenge DC

  • Success Point Threshold

Step 2: PCs make Checks

Each Player describes what their PC is attempting to contribute toward the Challenge. The GM will then assign a Check to that description or list multiple options for the PC to choose from. Players can request alternative Checks if what the GM called for didn’t match with what they had in mind. This process is designed to be collaborative between GM and PCs.

Step 3: Success Points Earned

Each Player’s Check result determines the number of Success Points they earn for their party as shown below:

  • Success: PC earns 1 Success Point, + 1 additional Success Point if it’s a Critical Success.

  • Success (each 5): PC earns 1 additional Success Point.

  • Failure: The player earns no Success Points, and potentially a penalty.

  • Critical Failure: The player causes the party to lose 1 Success Point

Step 4: Abjudicate Results

Determine Individual Success/Fail and Party Success/ Fail results

Additional Rules

Resources: PCs don’t spend resources (such as MP, SP, etc.) in Challenges.

Repeated Checks: If a PC repeats the same narrative action and uses the same Check as another PC, that Check is made with DisADV.

Helping Allies: PCs might want to help an ally instead of performing their own specific action. This is totally fine, but the PC must still make a Check that relates to how they’re helping their ally. They can’t simply grant them a Help Die or anything along those lines.

ADV & DisADV: PCs who have permanent or situational ADV or DisADV on a certain Check due to Features, Spells, or Equipment make that Check with ADV or DisADV as they would normally. If the Challenge presents a unique situation where the GM feels that this ADV or DisADV wouldn’t apply, they can rule that the Player ignores it for that Check.

Mental Acumen: PCs show off their knowledge.

Wilderness Survival: PCs show off their ability to survive in nature.

This is a three-step guide for how to generally run a combat.

Each PC must make an Initiative Check against the Encounter DC. To make an Initiative Check, roll a d20 and add your Combat Mastery and Agility to the result. Bonuses and Penalties to Agility Checks don’t affect Initiative Checks, unless otherwise stated.

Individual Outcomes

Use the following Success and Failure Statements to determine the outcome of your Initiative Check:

  • Success: You gain a d6 Inspiration Die, which you can add to 1 Check or Save of your choice that you make during this Combat. The Inspiration Die expires when the Combat ends

  • Critical Success: You gain ADV on 1 Check or Save of your choice during the first Round of Combat.

  • Critical Failure: The first Attack made against you during the first Round of Combat has ADV.

Party Outcomes

Use the following Success and Failure Statements to determine the outcome of your Initiative Check:

Use the following Success and Failure Statements to determine who will take the first turn in Combat.

  • Success: If at least 1/2 the PCs beat the Encounter DC, the PC Team wins the first Initiative Turn of the Combat.

  • Failure: If less than 1/2 the PCs beat the Encounter DC, the Enemy Team wins the first Initiative Turn of the Combat.

An Initiative Turn is a creature’s opportunity to act during Combat, allowing them to move, attack, cast spells, or take other available actions.

Initiative Turns are arranged so that each Team takes one Turn at a time, so when a member of one Team takes a Turn, they are followed by a member of the other Team taking a Turn. This continues back and forth until each member of each Team has taken a Turn.

At the start of each PC Turn during the first Round of Combat, the PC Team will decide which PC will take that Turn. If multiple PCs want to take the same Turn, the PC among them with the highest Initiative Check takes the Turn.

Shared PC Turns: If 2 or more PCs have the same Initiative Check result, they can choose to share a Turn. When they do, they act at the same time in the Initiative Order. They can choose which of them acts first or alternate their actions as desired.

Enemies can each be assigned to their own Enemy Turns, grouped together into a single Enemy Turn, or divided into multiple Enemy Turns in any combination the GM chooses.

Once each creature has taken their first Turn in Combat, the first Round of Combat is completed, and the next Round can begin. Combat progresses, Round by Round, until the PCs or their enemies prove victorious, and the GM declares that Combat has ended.

A Round of Combat lasts 12 in-game seconds, so 5 Rounds equals 1 minute of in-game time. You can imagine each creature’s Turn happening near simultaneously to each other with some creatures acting split-seconds before others.

Having survived a series of encounters, you have the chance to settle down for a Quick Rest. Quick Rests take 10 minutes. and you can be mildly active during the Rest but can’t do anything strenuous. You remember that you need to get back to the pick-up location before dark. Do you want to Rest now?

Rest Points

During a Rest PCs can expend Rest Points, regaining 1 HP per Rest Point they spend.

DC Tip: Feel free to add as much roleplay and extra flavor here as you’d like. The descriptions here are left to the bare minimum to give you the freedom to improvise how any ending conversations go as the adventure draws to a close.
This adventure can easily provide more plot hooks for your party to interact with down the line. Described below are potential plot hooks you can take and run with, to make your own evolving story.

Hearing the trouble involving Raven and her goons brings concerns of other fractures within the guild. Groups that were allied with The Shadows might seek revenge on the party, or the Bloodsworn may ask the party to root out other corrupt members.

With their success on their mission, and taking out a krasis along with their original target, the party is given some renown within the Bloodsworn. Eugene takes the party under his wing and provides them with more contracts for monsters to hunt. If the party continues along this path, they may just become the guild’s newest Reapers.

By slaying the krasis and its young, the party has upset the balance of nature’s cycle in the swamp. A massive sleeping serpent that wakes once every 10 years to hunt krasis awakens to find its food supply dwindling. However, it finds that travelers through its territory may be enough to sate its appetite before it slumbers again....

Each of the powers which initially founded Wildfang keeps a small military presence in the city, and after a scuffle between members of two different organizations, a soldier from one encampment was later found dead. The deputies serving as ambassadors for these powers do not trust the impartiality of the investigator assigned to the case, and so turn to the Bloodsworn for aid - a job which might be suited for the party.
